expanded_description: Master the transition from two-dimensional geometry to three-dimensional space by identifying polyhedra and curved solids, understanding their defining features (faces, edges, vertices), and learning to interpret and construct nets as accurate 2D unfoldings of 3D figures.

core_concept.explanation: Every three-dimensional solid can be mentally or physically 'unfolded' into a flat, two-dimensional pattern called a net. A valid net must contain all faces of the solid, connect them along matching edges, and fold without gaps or overlaps. Recognizing how 2D polygons assemble into 3D space forms is the foundational skill for surface area calculation, engineering design, and spatial reasoning.

image.alt: Side-by-side visualization: a 3D rectangular prism on the left, its unfolded 2D net on the right with fold lines marked
image.caption: Nets preserve face shapes and edge lengths while flattening spatial depth into planar layout

sections.section_title: Space Figures: Faces, Edges, and Vertices

image.alt: 3D cube and triangular pyramid with faces, edges, and vertices clearly labeled and color-coded
image.caption: Faces are flat surfaces, edges are line intersections, vertices are corner points

sections.introduction: Before working with nets or surface area, you must accurately classify and describe 3D figures. Space figures are categorized by whether their surfaces are polygons (polyhedra) or contain curves (cylinders, cones, spheres).

key_concept.definition: A space figure is a 3D shape occupying volume. Polyhedra have flat polygonal faces: prisms have two congruent parallel bases and rectangular lateral faces; pyramids have one polygonal base and triangular lateral faces meeting at an apex. Non-polyhedral solids include cylinders, cones, and spheres.
key_concept.context: Euler's Polyhedron Formula ($V - E + F = 2$) holds for all convex polyhedra, providing a quick verification tool for face/edge/vertex counts.

image.alt: Formula graphic with V, E, F defined visually next to a polyhedron example
image.caption: Euler's formula connects the three fundamental counts of any convex polyhedron

principles.principle: Classification by Base and Lateral Faces
principles.statement: Prisms are named by their base shape (triangular prism, hexagonal prism). Pyramids are named by their base shape (square pyramid, pentagonal pyramid). All lateral faces connect to form the vertical or sloping surface.
principles.application: Identify the base first, count its sides ($n$), then apply formulas: Prism has $n+2$ faces, $3n$ edges, $2n$ vertices. Pyramid has $n+1$ faces, $2n$ edges, $n+1$ vertices.
principles.example: Triangular prism: $n=3 \Rightarrow$ 5 faces, 9 edges, 6 vertices. Square pyramid: $n=4 \Rightarrow$ 5 faces, 8 edges, 5 vertices.
principles.analogy: Like stacking or capping a base: prisms stack a duplicate, pyramids cap it to a point.

image.alt: Side-by-side comparison showing base duplication vs base convergence to apex
image.caption: Base structure dictates the entire solid's topology

worked_examples.title: Applying Euler's Formula for Verification
worked_examples.problem: A convex polyhedron has 8 vertices and 12 edges. How many faces does it have, and what solid could it be?

step_by_step.calculation: 6 faces, 8 vertices, 12 edges matches a rectangular prism (cube or cuboid).

worked_examples.concept_applied: Euler's Polyhedron Formula for topological verification

image.alt: Polyhedron with labeled counts, calculation flow annotated
image.caption: Euler's formula instantly validates or reveals unknown face/edge/vertex counts

image.alt: Multiple nets shown: one valid cube net with fold lines, one invalid net with overlapping faces, arrows indicating folding direction
image.caption: A valid net must have exactly one face per solid side, edge lengths must match, and folding must not cause overlaps

sections.introduction: Nets are 2D patterns that fold into 3D solids. Recognizing valid nets requires understanding face adjacency, edge pairing, and spatial folding rules. Not every arrangement of polygons forms a valid net.

key_concept.definition: A net is a 2D arrangement of polygons connected along their edges that can be folded along those edges to form a closed 3D figure without gaps or overlaps.
key_concept.context: Many solids have multiple valid nets (a cube has 11 distinct nets). Validity depends on connectivity and folding geometry, not just having the correct number of faces.

image.alt: Grid showing all 11 possible cube nets arranged in a 3-4-4 layout
image.caption: Spatial flexibility creates multiple valid unfoldings for the same solid

classifications.value: Criteria that determine if a 2D arrangement folds into a valid solid
characteristics: All faces connect in a single continuous piece
Folding angles allow closure without gaps
classifications.behavior: Test nets mentally by picking a base, folding adjacent faces up, and checking if remaining faces meet correctly at edges/vertices.

examples.process: Identifying a valid triangular prism net
examples.explanation: A valid net has 2 congruent triangles and 3 rectangles. The rectangles must form a continuous strip between the triangles, or the triangles attach to opposite rectangles in the strip.

image.alt: Valid triangular prism net with rectangles in a row and triangles on opposite ends
image.caption: Base shapes and lateral band must align for successful folding

worked_examples.title: Net to Solid Identification
worked_examples.problem: A net contains one square base and four identical triangles attached to each side of the square. What 3D solid does it form, and how many faces, edges, and vertices will it have?

step_by_step.calculation: 1 square base + 4 triangular lateral faces meeting at a common point when folded.
This structure defines a square pyramid.

image.alt: Net folding animation frames showing triangles rising to form apex
image.caption: Lateral faces converging at a point always indicate a pyramid

image.alt: Four-step flowchart: Count faces -> Check edge matching -> Mental fold base -> Verify closure
image.caption: Systematic spatial reasoning prevents net identification errors

sections.introduction: Interpreting nets and visualizing 3D assembly requires a disciplined mental workflow. This framework trains spatial awareness and ensures accurate solid identification or net validation.

process_flow.description: Identify how many polygons are present and their shapes. Match counts to known solid face formulas ($n+2$ for prisms, $n+1$ for pyramids).

image.alt: Diagram with polygons highlighted and counts annotated
image.caption: Face count immediately narrows possible solids

process_flow.description: Check that connecting edges share identical lengths. Mismatched lengths indicate an invalid net or missing dimension data.

image.alt: Close-up of shared edges with tick marks showing equality
image.caption: Equal edge lengths are mandatory for clean folding

process_flow.process: Mental fold from a base face
process_flow.description: Select one face as the stationary base. Fold adjacent faces up 90° (prisms) or inward toward a center (pyramids). Track where remaining faces land.

image.alt: Arrow diagram showing rotational folding directions around base edges
image.caption: Base-first folding reduces cognitive load in mental rotation

process_flow.description: Ensure all faces meet perfectly at edges and vertices with no gaps or overlaps. Use Euler's formula to verify counts if needed.

image.alt: Completed solid with checkmark, net fold lines fading out
image.caption: Closure validation confirms geometric accuracy

worked_examples.title: Evaluating Net Validity for a Cube
worked_examples.problem: Determine if a net with 6 squares arranged in a 'T' shape (4 squares in a vertical column, 1 square attached to the left of the second square, 1 square attached to the right of the second square) folds into a cube.

step_by_step.calculation: 6 squares present. Matches cube face count.

step_by_step.explanation: Mental fold with center square as base
step_by_step.calculation: Top square folds up (back). Bottom square folds down (front). Left/right squares fold up (sides).
The sixth square attaches to the bottom of the column. When folded, it becomes the bottom face, completing the cube without overlap.

worked_examples.concept_applied: Spatial folding simulation and closure verification

image.alt: Step-by-step fold simulation of T-shaped net forming cube
image.caption: Systematic face tracking confirms net validity

categories.description: Systematic mistakes in net interpretation and spatial reasoning.
categories.detailed_mechanism: Assuming any arrangement of correct face counts works, ignoring edge length mismatches, misidentifying bases vs lateral faces, and failing to check for overlaps when multiple faces fold to the same position.

image.alt: Three warning panels showing overlapping fold, mismatched edges, and wrong base selection with corrections
image.caption: Recognizing these patterns prevents spatial reasoning failures

examples.explanation: Error: Four squares in a straight line with one square on each end. When folded, the end squares overlap at the same face position. Correction: A cube net cannot have more than 4 squares in a single straight row without causing overlap.

image.alt: Invalid net highlighted with red overlap zone vs valid rearrangement
image.caption: Linear alignment limits folding capacity

examples.explanation: Error: Student calls a pyramid with rectangular base a 'prism' because it has 4 triangular sides. Correction: Prisms have two parallel congruent bases. Pyramids have one base and triangular faces meeting at an apex. Face count alone is insufficient; topology matters.
examples.note: Always trace connectivity: Do faces meet at a point (pyramid) or form a parallel pair (prism)?

summary: Space figures are three-dimensional solids classified by their surfaces: polyhedra (prisms and pyramids with polygonal faces) and curved solids (cylinders, cones, spheres). Every space figure is defined by its faces, edges, and vertices, which obey Euler's Formula ($V - E + F = 2$) for convex polyhedra. A net is a 2D unfolded pattern that reconstructs the 3D solid when folded along shared edges without gaps or overlaps. Valid nets must have matching edge lengths, correct face counts, and foldable connectivity. To analyze nets and solids: (1) count and classify faces, (2) verify edge compatibility, (3) simulate folding from a chosen base, and (4) check for complete closure. Common errors include assuming correct face counts guarantee validity, ignoring edge length mismatches, and misidentifying pyramids vs prisms based solely on side counts. Mastering spatial visualization and net interpretation provides the essential foundation for surface area calculation, geometric modeling, and real-world applications in packaging, architecture, and 3D design.
